transcript.text: I keep postponing the actual work, staring at debt numbers all day, and still can't decide on a next move. My former co‑founder is upset that I'm not keeping up, and I'm just feeling like a ghost in the office. With Mom gone, the whole thing feels like a pivot with no roadmap.

You're feeling the weight of your mom's absence, and it's like the whole office has become a reminder of what's not working. The debt numbers are staring you down, and your former co-founder's expectations are adding to the pressure. It's no wonder you're feeling stuck - it's like you're trying to navigate a pivot without a roadmap. What's the smallest thing that you think might help you move forward, even if it's just a tiny step?

I’ve been staring at the spreadsheet all day, and every time I think I can make a move I just end up scrolling through old emails. Mom’s funeral was a pivot point, but the real pivot is figuring out how to pay the debt without losing the team members who are already angry. I don’t even know the smallest step—maybe I could just write down a list of options, but that feels like another task.

You're sitting at your desk, staring at a spreadsheet that's not giving you any answers, and every time you think you can make a move, you get pulled back into old emails. It's like you're trying to find a way out of this overwhelming situation, but every door you try leads to more questions. The debt, the team's anger, the weight of your mom's absence - it's all piling up, and it's no wonder you feel like you're not even sure where to start. Maybe just taking a step back and writing down a list of options, no matter how small they seem, could be a way to begin finding some clarity.

I tried drafting that list, but it just turned into a spreadsheet of options I can't decide on. The co‑founder still calls me a ghost, and I'm stuck in this loop of guilt and grief. Maybe I should try a quick call with him, but I'm not sure he'll even listen.

You're still feeling the weight of your mom's absence and the pressure from your co-founder, and it's like you're trying to find a way out of this overwhelming situation, but every door you try leads to more questions. Drafting that list of options was a great idea, but it's turned into another source of uncertainty. I can see why you're hesitant to reach out to your co-founder, but at the same time, it might be a way to break the loop of guilt and grief. What's the worst that could happen if you do try to call him, and what's the best that could come out of it?

Worst case, he’ll call me a ghost again and I’ll just sink deeper into guilt; best case, we clear the air and I get some direction. Mom’s gone, so I feel like I’m navigating a pivot with no map. I’m tempted to just write a quick note before the call, but I’m not sure if that counts as a step.

You're really scared that your co-founder will dismiss you again, and that fear is holding you back from making this call. At the same time, you're hoping that talking to him might give you some direction and help you clear the air. It's like you're standing at a crossroads, unsure which path to take. Writing a quick note before the call might be a way to clarify your thoughts and feel more prepared - does that feel like it could be a helpful step to you?
I’m not sure a note will change anything—I keep postponing the call like the rest of the tasks, and Mom’s absence still feels like an endless weight.

The weight of your mom's absence is still feeling like a huge burden, and it's no wonder you're struggling to make decisions about the business. You've been putting off the call to your co-founder, and even the idea of writing a note before it feels uncertain. It sounds like you're feeling really stuck, and I want to acknowledge that it's okay to feel that way. What's been the hardest part of considering this call, is it the fear of your co-founder's reaction or something else?

Honestly, it's not just fear of his reaction—it's the guilt of feeling like I've let everyone down, especially after Mom's passing. I keep putting off the call, just like everything else. I don't even know if reaching out is worth it.

You're carrying a heavy burden of guilt, feeling like you've let everyone down, and that's making it hard to take action, even something as simple as making a call to your co-founder. The weight of your mom's absence is still feeling overwhelming, and it's like every decision and action is being filtered through that grief. I want to acknowledge how tough this must be for you, and how it's affecting every part of your life. Can you tell me more about what's holding you back from reaching out to your co-founder - is it the fear of his reaction, or is there something else at play?

Honestly, every time I think about calling him, I just freeze and put it off again—like the guilt is a wall I can't climb over. Mom’s gone and that grief is a constant background noise, and it feels like I’m just stuck in this limbo of my own making. I’m not sure if the call will help or just make me feel worse, so I keep delaying it.

The guilt you're carrying is a heavy burden, and it's no wonder you're freezing when you think about calling your co-founder. It's like that guilt is a wall that's blocking your path forward. I can see how the grief of losing your mom is still a constant presence in your life, and it's affecting every decision you make. Yeah, it's really tough to know what to do when you're feeling stuck like this. Can you tell me more about what you think might happen if you do make the call, and what's the worst-case scenario that's playing out in your mind?

I keep picturing him saying I'm a ghost again, and that just feels like another blow to an already battered ego. The worst is that I get shut down, the debt stays, and the guilt just piles on, making it impossible to move forward. If I do call, I'm terrified that it will just add to the grief and leave me stuck in this limbo.

The image of your co-founder saying you're a ghost again is a really painful one, and it's no wonder you're terrified of being shut down and feeling like you're stuck in this limbo. It sounds like the fear of rejection is a major obstacle for you right now. Can you tell me more about what you think you need to happen in order to feel like you can move forward, even if it's just a small step?

I keep thinking a "small step" is just scheduling a call, but the debt spreadsheet keeps telling me otherwise. The weight of Mom's loss, the debt, and my co‑founder's anger feel like a single KPI I can't improve. Maybe I should set a hard deadline for the call, but the fear of being shut down keeps me postponing.

The debt spreadsheet can be a harsh reminder of your struggles, and it's no wonder you're feeling like you're stuck. Setting a hard deadline for the call might be a way to take control, but I can see how the fear of being shut down is holding you back. What's the smallest step you could take right now to start moving forward, even if it's just a tiny one?

I set a deadline, but every time I check the clock it feels like a reminder of how much I’m still stuck. The call feels like a big KPI that I can’t hit, and Mom’s absence is the constant background noise that keeps me from even starting. I think maybe I’ll just send a short text first, but even that seems like a big step.

The weight of your mom's absence is still feeling like a huge burden, and it's no wonder you're struggling to take action. Setting a deadline for the call was a great idea, but I can see how the pressure of it is making you feel even more stuck. Sending a short text first might be a good way to start small, but even that feels like a big step right now. What if we broke it down even further - what's the smallest thing you could do today to start moving forward, even if it's just a tiny step?

My former co‑founder is upset that I'm not keeping up, and I'm just feeling like a ghost in the office. With Mom gone, the whole thing feels like a pivot with no roadmap.

My entry point will be to acknowledge the pain and uncertainty you're feeling, and see if we can't find a way to break through the paralysis that's holding you back.

response.final_response: The guilt you're carrying is a heavy burden, and it's no wonder you're freezing when you think about calling your co-founder. response.final_response: The weight of your mom's absence is still feeling like a huge burden, and it's no wonder you're struggling to take action.
